title: Unauthenticated proxy to Qiwa government API (/api/qiwa)
description: The REST route apps/web/app/api/qiwa/route.ts exposes GET, POST and PUT handlers that acquire a Qiwa OAuth token and proxy the request straight to the Qiwa government API (api.qiwa.sa / sandbox). No auth() session check, no role check, no tenant scoping and no Zod validation are performed. The path is also NOT included in middleware.ts config.matcher, so the global rate-limit and same-origin CSRF checks never run for it. The request body is forwarded verbatim to the Qiwa endpoint (JSON.stringify(body)) and the employee id comes from an unvalidated ?id= query param. A properly gated tRPC equivalent (qiwaRouter, requireRole(...QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES)) already exists, so this is an unprotected parallel door to the same integration.
expectedBehavior: Every call to a government-integration endpoint must require an authenticated session, enforce the qiwa manage roles, be tenant-scoped, validate the payload with the existing qiwaEmployeeSchema, and be covered by rate limiting.
actualBehavior: Any unauthenticated caller can POST/PUT arbitrary employee data to Qiwa or GET employee records, using the server's own Qiwa credentials, with no session, role, tenant, validation or rate limit.
reproduction: curl -X POST https://<host>/api/qiwa -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"fullName":"x"}'  (no cookie/session) -> request is proxied to Qiwa and the raw Qiwa response is returned.
recommendation: Add auth() + role check (QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES) at the top of every handler, resolve tenant from the session, validate the body with qiwaEmployeeSchema/qiwaContractSchema, add /api/qiwa to middleware.matcher for rate-limit + CSRF, and return generic error messages. Preferably retire this REST route in favour of the already-gated tRPC qiwaRouter.
workaround: Block /api/qiwa at the edge/WAF or delete the route until authentication is added.

title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant data exposure (/api/auth/check-tenants)
category: Broken Access Control / Excessive Data Exposure
description: apps/web/app/api/auth/check-tenants/route.ts GET returns the 10 most recently created tenants (id, companyName, crNumber, nitaqatActivity, planTier, regulatoryContext, schemaName) and the 10 most recent users (id, email, name, role, tenantId) with NO authentication. This is the same registry data that the tRPC procedure auth.tenantsList deliberately gates behind a PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist (SEC-002) and which even omits schemaName. The route is not in middleware.matcher, so it is not even rate limited.
expectedBehavior: Cross-tenant registry data (customer company names, Saudi CR numbers, internal Postgres schema names, and user emails/roles) must only be readable by an authenticated platform operator, matching the auth.tenantsList gate.
actualBehavior: Anyone on the internet can enumerate customer companies, CR numbers, internal schema names, and user emails/roles across ALL tenants by requesting one URL.
reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants  -> JSON dump of recent tenants (incl crNumber + schemaName) and users (incl email + role).
recommendation: Delete this diagnostic route, or gate it behind the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist used by auth.tenantsList and add it to middleware.matcher. Never return schemaName to any client.
workaround: Remove the route file immediately; it is redundant with auth.tenantsList.

title: Tenant provisioning DDL creates only 2 of ~24 module tables — new tenants are structurally broken
description: generateTenantDDL() emits CREATE TABLE for only `departments` and `employees`. The tenant schema module defines ~24 tables (payroll_runs, payslips, wage_files, expenses, leave_types/requests/balances, attendance shifts/records/exceptions, documents, compliance_checks, final_settlements, notifications, audit_logs, recruitment, retention, ai, qiwa_contracts, policy_documents, guide_maps, employee_invitations, employment_history). None of the other tables are created when a tenant is provisioned at runtime.
expectedBehavior: createTenantSchema() should materialize the complete, current tenant table set (every table exported from schema/tenant/index.ts) with all constraints, indexes and triggers, so all modules work for a freshly signed-up tenant.
actualBehavior: Only departments + employees are created. Payroll, leave, attendance, expenses, recruitment, audit, compliance etc. tables do not exist for a new tenant, so those modules fail with 'relation does not exist' until someone hand-runs a seed/DDL script.
reproduction: Call createTenantRegistry(company, cr, activity) (the production signup path) then connect to the new tenant_<hex> schema and \dt. Only departments and employees exist. Any payroll/leave/attendance query throws 42P01 relation does not exist.
recommendation: Generate tenant DDL from the Drizzle schema (single source of truth) rather than a hand-maintained string. Options: (a) drive drizzle-kit against each new tenant schema via SET search_path; (b) build a per-tenant migration runner that replays an authoritative ordered DDL bundle covering all tables + constraints + indexes + triggers; (c) at minimum, extend generateTenantDDL to the full table set and add a test asserting parity with schema/tenant/index.ts exports.
workaround: Run scripts/seed-rukn-modules.ts (PHASE 1 DDL) against each new tenant manually.
estimatedEffort: L (2-4 days: author authoritative per-tenant DDL/migration mechanism + parity test)

title: Migrations 0001 & 0002 hardcode a single dev tenant schema (tenant_1ed8b6bd3743) — changes never reach other or new tenants
description: Migration 0001 (immigration + gcc columns) and 0002 (policy_documents table + indexes) are written against a literal schema name `tenant_1ed8b6bd3743`. They ALTER/CREATE only inside that one developer tenant. Any other existing tenant and every future tenant never receives these columns/tables. Contrast with 0006/0007/0009 which correctly loop over information_schema.schemata LIKE 'tenant\_%'.
expectedBehavior: Schema migrations must apply to every tenant schema (loop over all tenant_<hex> schemas) and be baked into new-tenant provisioning.
actualBehavior: 0001/0002 mutate exactly one schema. Employees.immigration_status / visa_type / iqama_expiry / passport_expiry / occupation_code / skill_level / gcc_status and the policy_documents table exist only in tenant_1ed8b6bd3743.
reproduction: grep -roE 'tenant_[a-f0-9]{12}' packages/db/drizzle -> all hits are tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 in 0001 and 0002. Provision a second tenant, query employees.immigration_status -> column does not exist.
recommendation: Rewrite 0001/0002 as DO-loops over all tenant_<hex> schemas (as 0006/0007/0009 do) AND fold the columns/tables into the authoritative provisioning DDL from DB-001. Add a CI check that fails on any literal tenant_<hex> reference in migrations. Also fix the date-vs-text mismatch (see DB-005).
workaround: Manually replay the ALTERs against every existing tenant schema.

title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant registry disclosure via GET /api/auth/check-tenants (re-opens SEC-002, leaks schemaName)
description: The custom route GET /api/auth/check-tenants returns the ENTIRE tenant registry (companyName, crNumber, nitaqatActivity, planTier, regulatoryContext AND internal Postgres schemaName) plus the 10 most recent users across ALL tenants (email, name, role, tenantId) with NO authentication, no role check and no tenant scoping. This is the exact data SEC-002 was remediated for on the tRPC procedure auth.tenantsList (now gated to PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS with schemaName removed) — but this parallel REST door bypasses that fix entirely and additionally re-exposes schemaName, the internal isolation key SEC-002 explicitly removed. The path is not in the middleware matcher, so no rate-limit or edge guard applies either.
expectedBehavior: Cross-tenant registry data must be restricted to a true platform operator (same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS boundary used by auth.tenantsList) and must never expose schemaName. Ideally this diagnostic route should be deleted.
actualBehavior: Any anonymous HTTP client receives every tenant's company name, CR number, plan tier and internal schemaName plus recent user emails/roles/tenantIds.
reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants -> 200 JSON listing all tenants (incl. schemaName) and recent users across every tenant, no cookie/token supplied.
recommendation: Delete apps/web/app/api/auth/check-tenants/route.ts. If a diagnostic is required, gate it on the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS operator boundary as auth.tenantsList and never return schemaName. Add the path to the middleware matcher regardless.
workaround: Block /api/auth/check-tenants at the edge/CDN until removed.

title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant disclosure of users and tenants via GET /api/auth/check-tenants
category: Broken Access Control / Sensitive Data Exposure
description: The check-tenants route handler performs no authentication or authorization and is not covered by the middleware matcher, so any anonymous caller can enumerate the 10 most recent tenants (company name, CR number, schema name, plan tier, regulatory context) and the 10 most recent users (email, name, role, tenantId) across the ENTIRE registry - a cross-tenant PII and infrastructure-metadata leak.
expectedBehavior: A registry-wide listing of tenants and users must require an authenticated platform-operator (the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist gate used by authRouter.tenantsList), and must never be reachable anonymously.
actualBehavior: GET /api/auth/check-tenants returns tenant + user records with no session check. The route is absent from middleware.ts `config.matcher`, so even the middleware never runs for it.
reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants -> returns JSON with cross-tenant tenants[] (companyName, crNumber, schemaName) and users[] (email, name, role, tenantId), no auth required.
recommendation: Delete the route or gate it behind the platform-operator allowlist (reuse the PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS check from trpc/routers/auth.ts tenantsList) and add it to the middleware matcher. Return 404 to anonymous callers.
workaround: Block /api/auth/check-tenants at the edge/CDN until fixed.

title: Expense self-approval: submitter can set themselves as the approver and approve their own claim
role: department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter (any non-employee staff role)
description: createExpenseSchema accepts a client-supplied approverEmployeeId with no check that it differs from the submitter. The approve mutation authorises the row's approverEmployeeId (or any HR role). A submitter who sets approverEmployeeId to their own employee id therefore becomes the approver of their own expense and can approve it. Plain 'employee' role is blocked from expense.approve by canAccessProcedure, but every staff role above employee (department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter) reaches approve via protectedProcedure.
expectedBehavior: An expense approver must be a different person from the submitter (actor != target). A self-referential approver should be rejected at submission, and approve must refuse when approverEmployeeId === the row's employeeId.
actualBehavior: No actor != target guard exists. approverEmployeeId is taken verbatim from input; approve only checks that the caller is the assigned approver or HR, so a self-assigned approver passes.
reproduction: As a department_manager, call expense.create with approverEmployeeId = own employeeId and amount = 5000. Then call expense.approve { id, action: 'approve' }. The row moves to 'approved' with no second person involved.
recommendation: Reject approverEmployeeId === submitter at create; in approve, additionally block when existing.employeeId === existing.approverEmployeeId or when the acting HR user is the submitter. Enforce a two-person rule for any monetary approval.
workaround: Restrict approve/markPaid to a dedicated finance role and monitor the audit log for same-actor submit+approve pairs (not currently logged — see BIZ-003).

title: audit_logs table and its append-only trigger are not part of tenant provisioning — new tenants have no audit trail and no tamper protection
description: generateTenantDDL does not create audit_logs, so new tenants have no audit table at all. Migration 0007 installs the append-only BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger only for schemas that ALREADY contain audit_logs, and runs once at migration time. A tenant created after 0007 was applied therefore gets neither the table nor the trigger. The trigger design itself is correct and idempotent, but it is not wired into provisioning.
expectedBehavior: Every tenant should be created with audit_logs plus the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger so audit entries are immutable from day one.
actualBehavior: New tenants: no audit_logs table (audit writes fail or are skipped). Even if the table were added later by a script, the append-only trigger is not re-installed for tenants created after 0007.
reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \dt shows no audit_logs. Add the table by hand and note no audit_logs_no_update/no_delete trigger exists -> UPDATE/DELETE on audit rows succeeds.
recommendation: Add audit_logs (with actor/action/target/before/after/ip/timestamp) and the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger to the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0007 as the backfill for legacy schemas. Add an integration test asserting UPDATE/DELETE on audit_logs raises in a freshly provisioned tenant.
workaround: Run the 0007 DO-block after every tenant creation.

title: Provisioned employees table is missing immigration/gcc columns the app requires, and migration types (text) diverge from schema (date)
description: The Drizzle employees schema defines immigration columns (passportExpiry, iqamaExpiry, exitReentryExpiry as `date`; visaType, occupationCode, skillLevel, immigrationStatus, gccStatus). generateTenantDDL omits all of these. Migration 0001 adds them ONLY to tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 (see DB-002) AND declares passport_expiry/iqama_expiry/exit_reentry_expiry as `text`, conflicting with the `date` type in the canonical schema. So new tenants lack the columns; the one hardcoded tenant has them with the wrong type.
expectedBehavior: Provisioned employees table matches employees.ts exactly, including immigration columns with correct `date` types.
actualBehavior: New tenants: columns absent. Hardcoded tenant: columns present but typed text where Drizzle expects date -> type coercion / comparison bugs for expiry logic.
reproduction: Provision a new tenant, query employees.iqama_expiry -> column missing. On tenant_1ed8b6bd3743, inspect column type -> text, not date.
recommendation: Include immigration/gcc columns with correct date/enum types in the authoritative provisioning DDL; align 0001 to date types; backfill/convert text->date on the affected schema.
workaround: Manually add columns (as date) to affected tenant schemas.

title: Dashboard shell uses hardcoded physical left/padding-left, so layout does not mirror in RTL (Arabic)
description: globals.css states the intent explicitly: "RTL: every component is RTL-first; dir=\"rtl\" on <html> flips physical properties ... so layouts mirror automatically." DashboardShell does correctly toggle document.documentElement.dir/lang at runtime (dashboard-shell.tsx:35-36), but the shell's own layout uses hardcoded physical Tailwind classes instead of logical ones: the sidebar wrapper is pinned with `left-0` and the content column uses `md:pl-[278px]`. Neither responds to `dir="rtl"`.
expectedBehavior: When preferredLanguage is switched to Arabic (dir=rtl), the sidebar should move to the visual right edge and the main content column should reserve space on the right (e.g. via `inset-inline-start-0` / `ps-[278px]` logical utilities, or `[dir=rtl]:` overrides), matching the RTL-first design intent documented in globals.css.
actualBehavior: The sidebar stays pinned to the physical left of the viewport and main content keeps its left padding even after switching to Arabic, producing a left-docked nav in an otherwise right-to-left reading page.
reproduction: 1. Sign in to the dashboard. 2. Click the language toggle (Globe icon) in the header to switch to العربية. 3. Observe the sidebar remains on the left and main content padding is unchanged, rather than mirroring to the right.
recommendation: Replace `left-0`/`pl-[278px]` (and similar physical positioning in header.tsx, employees list search icon, etc.) with Tailwind logical-property utilities (`start-0`, `ps-[278px]`) or add `[dir=rtl]:right-0 [dir=rtl]:pr-[278px]` overrides, then visually QA every dashboard page in Arabic.
workaround: None for end users; Arabic users can still complete tasks, just with a mirrored-incorrectly nav rail.

title: Language toggle on the login page is non-functional (dead button)
description: The login page renders what looks like a working EN/Arabic toggle in the top bar, but the "العربية" element is a `<button>` with no `onClick` handler at all, and "EN" is a plain `<span>`, not an interactive control. Every other authenticated page in the app has a working language toggle (header.tsx uses `setPreferredLanguage`), so this looks like an oversight specific to the pre-login screen.
expectedBehavior: Clicking "العربية" switches the login page (and ideally the whole pre-auth flow) to Arabic/RTL, consistent with the working toggle used everywhere post-login.
actualBehavior: Clicking the "العربية" button does nothing; there is no state, no dir change, and no visual feedback of any kind.
reproduction: 1. Visit /login while signed out. 2. Click "العربية" in the top-right corner. 3. Nothing happens — page stays in English/LTR.
recommendation: Wire the login-page toggle to the same `Lang`/dir-switching mechanism used in DashboardShell, or at minimum route users to a `?lang=ar` variant of the login form with translated copy.
workaround: Users can complete login in English and switch language after signing in.

title: Custom /api/auth/login is a brute-force oracle (no rate limit, no Zod)
description: apps/web/app/api/auth/login/route.ts performs a raw bcrypt password comparison and returns the user's id/email/role/tenantId. It only checks that email and password are present (no Zod, no email-format or length bounds). The middleware rate limiter and per-account lockout are keyed on /api/auth/callback/credentials (the NextAuth path); this custom /api/auth/login route is NOT in middleware.matcher, so it has no rate limiting and no account lockout - a standalone unthrottled password-checking oracle.
expectedBehavior: All credential-verification endpoints should be rate limited per IP/account, enforce account lockout, and validate input. Ideally there should be a single authentication path (NextAuth).
actualBehavior: Attackers can brute-force passwords against /api/auth/login at full speed with no throttling or lockout; generic error text is good but the endpoint still confirms valid credentials and returns role/tenantId.
reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with a fixed email and a password list; observe no 429 throttling and no lockout after many attempts.
recommendation: Either remove this route in favour of NextAuth, or add it to middleware.matcher with the login rate-limit bucket, wire it to the same per-account lockout used in authorize(), and validate the body with a Zod schema (emailSchema + bounded password).
workaround: Add /api/auth/login to middleware.matcher and reuse the login:<ip> rate-limit key.

title: employee.getById / employee.list over-select salary + PII and lack object-level scoping
description: employee.getById and employee.list run on companyProcedure, which only excludes the employee and candidate roles - recruiter and department_manager are admitted. Both return the whole employees row (no column projection), including plaintext salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and the *_enc PII columns (iqamaNumberEnc, passportNumberEnc, bankIbanEnc). Unlike employee.list (which department-scopes a department_manager), employee.getById performs NO department scoping, so a department_manager or recruiter can fetch ANY employee's full record - including salary - by iterating UUIDs. This overlaps RBAC-004 (recruiter/department_manager must not see salary) but is reported here as a data-layer over-selection + missing object-level authorization issue.
expectedBehavior: Reads should project only the columns the caller is entitled to (salary and *_enc PII stripped for recruiter/department_manager) and getById should enforce the same department scoping as list.
actualBehavior: getById returns the entire employee row (salary + encrypted PII + payslips relation) to any company role with no department scoping; a recruiter sees salary they are explicitly not permitted to see.
reproduction: As a recruiter or department_manager, call trpc employee.getById with any employee UUID -> response includes salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and *_enc columns; the target need not be in the manager's department.
recommendation: Add a column allowlist (Drizzle columns projection) that strips salary and *_enc fields for non-payroll roles, and apply getManagedDepartmentIds scoping to employee.getById as is already done in employee.list. Coordinate with the RBAC owner (RBAC-004).
workaround: Restrict getById/list to the salary-authorized roles until per-column projection is added.

title: Orphan POST /api/auth/login is an unthrottled password-verification oracle (no rate limit, no lockout, no MFA)
description: A second, custom credentials endpoint exists alongside the NextAuth flow. It validates the password with bcrypt but is NOT in the middleware matcher (so the 5/min login rate limit never applies), does NOT consult or increment the durable account-lockout state, and does NOT enforce MFA. It therefore permits unlimited online password guessing against real accounts and, on success, discloses the account role and tenantId. The frontend uses signIn()/callback/credentials instead, so this route is unreferenced dead code that still serves live traffic.
expectedBehavior: All credential-checking endpoints must share the same brute-force controls (IP rate limit + durable per-account lockout + MFA gate) as the NextAuth authorize() path, or not exist.
actualBehavior: The route calls bcrypt compare and returns 200/401 with no throttling, no lockout accounting, and no MFA. It is missing from middleware.ts matcher entirely.
reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with {email, password} candidates - no 429 and no account lockout is ever triggered; a 200 confirms a valid credential pair and reveals role/tenantId.
recommendation: Delete this route (the app authenticates via NextAuth). If a JSON login API is genuinely needed, route it through the same lockout + rate-limit + MFA logic and add it to the middleware matcher.
workaround: Add /api/auth/login to the middleware matcher with the login rate limit, or block it at the edge.

title: Monthly leave accrual compounds: year-to-date days are added to the existing balance on every run
description: calculateAccrual computes daysAccrued = (daysAllowed/12) * monthsElapsed, where monthsElapsed = min(12, currentMonth+1) is the YEAR-TO-DATE month count, then sets newBalance = previousBalance + daysAccrued. Because the YTD figure is ADDED to the already-accrued balance every time the job runs, running the monthly cron each month compounds the balance to daysAllowed * (1+2+...+12)/12 = daysAllowed * 6.5 by December (e.g. 21 days -> ~136 days). Correct only if run exactly once per year.
expectedBehavior: A monthly accrual run should add one month's entitlement (daysAllowed/12) to the balance, and be idempotent per month (no double-credit if re-run).
actualBehavior: It adds the YTD accrual to the prior balance, so periodic runs over-accrue geometrically.
reproduction: Seed balance 0. Run runMonthlyAccrual in Feb (monthsElapsed=2) -> balance = daysAllowed*2/12. Run again in Mar (monthsElapsed=3) -> balance = prev + daysAllowed*3/12. Balance grows by the YTD amount each month.
recommendation: Make the monthly path grant exactly one month (daysAccrued = monthlyAccrual) and add idempotency keyed to (employee, leaveType, year, month) so a re-run in the same month is a no-op; add unit tests asserting balance == entitlement after 12 monthly runs.
workaround: Run the accrual job at most once per year until fixed; verify balances manually.

title: iqama unique index and performance indexes are one-time backfills, not part of provisioning — new tenants can store duplicate national IDs and run unindexed
description: The partial unique index employees_iqama_unique (0009) and all hot-path indexes (0006) are applied by one-time DO-loops over existing schemas. generateTenantDDL creates employees.iqama_number_enc as plain TEXT with no unique index and none of the perf indexes. New tenants therefore lack DB-level duplicate-iqama protection and lack indexes on notifications, employees, leave_*, documents, payroll, payslips, expenses, final_settlements, compliance_checks, departments, attendance.
expectedBehavior: Provisioning should create the iqama partial unique index and the standard performance indexes for every tenant.
actualBehavior: Only tenants existing when 0006/0009 ran have them; new tenants have neither.
reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \di shows no employees_iqama_unique and no perf indexes. Insert two employees with the same iqama via raw SQL -> both succeed.
recommendation: Fold all indexes (incl. the iqama partial unique index) into the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0006/0009 as legacy backfills.
workaround: Replay 0006 and 0009 DO-blocks after each tenant creation.

title: employees table has zero explicit indexes despite being filtered by departmentId/employmentStatus/managerEmployeeId on nearly every query
page: Employees list, Departments, Attendance, Leave, Payroll (any screen scoped by employee/department)
description: packages/db/src/schema/tenant/employees.ts defines the employees table with no index()/uniqueIndex() calls at all. This table is the most heavily filtered/joined table in the app: employee.ts filters on departmentId and employmentStatus and sorts by fullName/hireDate/createdAt (apps/web/trpc/routers/employee.ts:18-37); attendance.ts filters employees by departmentId (line 459) and joins via managerEmployeeId for org-tree walks; leave.ts filters active employees by employmentStatus (line 337); departments.ts joins on headEmployeeId. Postgres does not auto-index foreign-key columns (unlike the primary key), so every one of these WHERE/JOIN clauses does a sequential scan once tenant employee counts grow past a few hundred rows. Only 7 of 33 schema files (packages/db/src/schema/**) define any explicit index at all; employees.ts and leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts are all in the no-index list.
expectedBehavior: Foreign-key / frequently-filtered columns (departmentId, employmentStatus, managerEmployeeId) should have explicit btree indexes so list/report queries scale with tenant size.
actualBehavior: No index definitions exist on the employees table or on most other tenant tables that are filtered by employeeId/departmentId in routers.
reproduction: Static evidence only — grep packages/db/src/schema for `index(`: only attendance.ts, shift-related and 6 other files define indexes; employees.ts, leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts have none.
recommendation: Add btree indexes on employees.departmentId, employees.employmentStatus, employees.managerEmployeeId, and the employeeId foreign key on leave_requests/expenses/documents/payslips/notifications; regenerate a Drizzle migration (pnpm db:generate) rather than hand-editing SQL.
workaround: None at scale currently deployed (demo tenant is small so this is not yet visible in production).

title: Tenant-isolation regression tests are mostly skipped, not executing
description: The vitest run shows packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-isolation.test.ts as "4 tests | 3 skipped" and packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-manager.test.ts as "5 tests | 4 skipped" — i.e. the majority of the tests specifically named for verifying cross-tenant data isolation are not actually executing in the current run (likely gated behind a live-DB precondition, consistent with the known-broken local DATABASE_URL noted in project memory).
expectedBehavior: Tenant-isolation tests are the most important regression guard in a multi-tenant SaaS (a prior RBAC audit already scored this area 90/100 after remediation) and should run on every CI build, not be silently skipped.
actualBehavior: 7 of 9 combined tests across the two tenant-isolation-related suites are skipped in this run.
reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm test — observe the skipped counts in the summary line for these two files.
recommendation: Confirm whether CI provisions a real DATABASE_URL so these suites run un-skipped there; if the skip is environment-conditional, add a CI-only Postgres service (e.g. via GitHub Actions services:) so tenant isolation is verified on every PR, not just locally when a DB happens to be available.
workaround: Tests exist and presumably pass when a DB is available; this is a coverage-execution gap, not a missing-test gap.

title: Expense markPaid is authorised by companyProcedure, letting non-finance roles pay out expenses
description: The markPaid mutation is documented as 'Only HR / super_admin can do this' but is guarded by companyProcedure, which only excludes the 'employee' and 'candidate' roles. Every other role, including recruiter (who has no finance function), can mark any approved expense as paid. There is no expenses:pay capability check. Combined with BIZ-001, a single department_manager can submit, approve, and pay one expense end-to-end.
expectedBehavior: Only a payroll/finance capability holder (e.g. payroll_admin, hr_manager, super_admin) should close out an expense as paid, and the payer should differ from the approver/submitter.
actualBehavior: companyProcedure permits department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin and recruiter; the code comment does not match the guard.
reproduction: As a recruiter, call expense.markPaid { id } for any approved expense. It succeeds and sets status='paid'.
recommendation: Replace companyProcedure with requireCapability of a new 'expenses:pay' (grant only to payroll_admin/hr_manager/super_admin), and add a payer != approver != submitter check. Write an audit record on payout.
workaround: Temporarily switch markPaid to requireRole('super_admin','hr_manager','payroll_admin').

title: Duplicate final settlement / EOSB: no uniqueness or status guard on settlement creation
description: settlement.create inserts a new final_settlements row on every call with no check for an existing settlement for the same employee, and the table has no unique constraint or 'status/paid' field. offboarding.initiate also inserts an independent final_settlements row. The same employee can therefore have multiple EOSB records, each a candidate for payout, with no idempotency or 'already settled' guard.
expectedBehavior: An employee should have at most one active final settlement; a repeat attempt should update the existing record or be rejected, and a settled/paid record must be immutable.
actualBehavior: Every create/initiate call inserts a fresh row; nothing prevents duplicates.
reproduction: Call settlement.create for employee X twice (or create then offboarding.initiate). Two final_settlements rows exist for X, each with an EOSB amount.
recommendation: Add a unique/active constraint on employeeId (or a settlement status), check for an existing settlement before insert, and make a paid settlement immutable. Consolidate offboarding.initiate to reuse/upsert the same row.
workaround: HR procedure to check for an existing settlement before creating one.

title: Real production Supabase DB connection string (with password) stored in cleartext file in repo working tree
description: _db_url.txt at the repo root contains a live Supabase pooler connection string including the database password and the tenant search_path (rukn_energy_services). The file is listed in .gitignore (so it is not committed to git history that was checked), but it sits in the developer working tree in plaintext, is trivially shared via backups/zips/screen-shares, and grants direct SQL access to production PII (iqama, IBAN, salary). A credential of this sensitivity must not live in a loose text file.
expectedBehavior: Production DB credentials live only in a secret manager / Vercel env vars, never in a working-tree file.
actualBehavior: Full production connection string with password is readable from _db_url.txt.
reproduction: Open _db_url.txt at repo root; the password is present in cleartext.
recommendation: Rotate the Supabase database password immediately, delete _db_url.txt from disk, and confirm it never entered git history (git log --all -- _db_url.txt). Store the URL only in the secret manager.
workaround: Delete the file now; rotate the credential regardless.

title: Password-reset token and user email written to server logs (account-takeover via log access)
description: The request-reset handler logs the full reset URL (which embeds the raw single-use reset token) together with the user's email via console.info. Server logs are typically retained and broadly readable (Vercel log drains, observability tools). Anyone with log access can read the token within its 1-hour TTL and complete an account takeover; the email is also PII exposed in logs. The DB correctly stores only the token hash, so the log is the weak link.
expectedBehavior: Reset links must be delivered only to the user's mailbox and never logged; at most log a non-sensitive event id.
actualBehavior: console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl) logs email + raw token.
reproduction: Call POST /api/auth/request-reset with a registered email; inspect server logs for the reset URL containing token and email.
recommendation: Wire the email delivery (TODO(email)) and remove the console.info entirely, or gate it behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' with the token redacted. Never log tokens or email.
workaround: Immediately restrict log access and set short log retention until fixed.

title: Unauthenticated proxy to Qiwa government API via /api/qiwa (re-opens SEC-009 through a REST twin)
description: The custom route /api/qiwa (POST/PUT/GET) authenticates to the Qiwa government platform with the server's QIWA_CLIENT_ID/SECRET and forwards the caller's raw JSON body to POST/PUT/GET https://api.qiwa.sa/api/v2/employees. It performs NO session check, NO role check and NO tenant resolution. The tRPC twin qiwa.sync was correctly re-gated to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) under SEC-009, but this REST endpoint is a wide-open parallel door to the same government actions (create / update / query employee records). It is also absent from the middleware matcher, so no rate-limit applies.
expectedBehavior: Any government-filing/integration action must require an authenticated session with integrations/payroll authority (requireRole super_admin/hr_manager/payroll_admin), mirroring qiwa.sync. Prefer removing the REST route and routing the UI through the guarded tRPC procedure.
actualBehavior: An anonymous request to /api/qiwa is proxied straight to the Qiwa government API using the tenant's credentials; if credentials are unset it returns 500 'Qiwa credentials not configured' (fail-open on authz, fail-closed only on missing creds).
reproduction: With QIWA creds configured: curl -X POST https://<host>/api/qiwa -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{...employee...}' (no cookie) -> forwarded to the Qiwa government platform.
recommendation: Delete apps/web/app/api/qiwa/route.ts (its capability is fully covered by qiwa.sync), or add auth()+requireRole gating at the top of each handler and register the path in the middleware matcher.
workaround: Block /api/qiwa at the edge and/or leave QIWA credentials unset until fixed.

title: attendance.getSubtree fetches every employee in the tenant on every call instead of the requested org subtree
page: Attendance guide map (org subtree with last-known GPS location)
description: The comment above the query says "Recursive CTE to get all employees under rootEmployeeId" but the actual Drizzle call has no `where` clause and no `limit`, so it loads the entire employees table (with department join) for the tenant on every request, then filters/builds the org tree in application memory (apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts:722-758). A second unbounded findMany over attendanceRecords (with: {}) then follows for every employee in that full result set.
expectedBehavior: Only employees within the requested rootEmployeeId's subtree (and their latest attendance record) should be fetched from the database, ideally via a recursive CTE or a scoped WHERE clause.
actualBehavior: ctx.db.query.employees.findMany({ with: { department: true } }) has no where/limit — it is a full tenant-wide employee scan on every guide-map request.
reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts lines 710-780 (getSubtree procedure) — no where clause is ever applied to the employees.findMany call before the in-memory tree/filter logic runs.
recommendation: Replace the full-table findMany with a recursive CTE (raw SQL via Drizzle's sql`` helper) or an iterative BFS that only expands children of rootEmployeeId, and add a limit as a safety cap.
workaround: None; acceptable today only because demo/pilot tenants are small.

title: Zero automated tests for the two largest business-logic routers: retention.ts (1611 lines) and recruitment.ts (812 lines)
page: Retention (goals, reviews, skills, succession, engagement, rewards, compensation), Recruitment (jobs, candidates, applications, interviews, offers, onboarding, referrals, background/reference checks)
description: The vitest run (152 tests passed across 26 suite files) contains no test file for apps/web/trpc/routers/retention.ts or apps/web/trpc/routers/recruitment.ts. These are the two single largest source files in the repo (1611 and 812 lines respectively) and implement RBAC-scoped CRUD + workflow logic for goals/OKRs, performance reviews, skills/succession, engagement surveys, rewards/compensation, and the full recruitment pipeline (candidates through onboarding). No __tests__ directory reference to `retention` or `recruitment` domain logic exists anywhere in packages/ or apps/web/src/__tests__.
expectedBehavior: Business-logic routers of this size, especially ones gating access by requireCapability/requireRole (payroll:view_company, performance:view_team, etc.), should have integration tests covering at least the RBAC gate and core CRUD/workflow paths.
actualBehavior: No test files reference these routers; coverage is 0% at the unit/integration level for both.
reproduction: find . -name '*.test.ts' | grep -i 'retention\|recruitment' — no results.
recommendation: Add integration tests per sub-router (goal, reviewCycle, skill, succession, engagement, reward, compensation for retention; job/candidate/application/interview/offer/onboarding/referral for recruitment) covering: RBAC gate rejection for unauthorized roles, pagination correctness, and at least one full create->read->update happy path per entity.
workaround: None.

title: pnpm audit: drizzle-orm pinned below the patched version for a high-severity SQL-injection advisory
description: `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` reports one high-severity advisory: "Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers" (GHSA-gpj5-g38j-94v9), affecting drizzle-orm <0.45.2. packages/db/package.json pins "drizzle-orm": "^0.38.0" and the resolved version is 0.38.4 (12 dependency paths). `pnpm audit` (no level filter) additionally reports 4 moderate advisories: esbuild <=0.24.2 (dev-server request/response exposure, via drizzle-kit's esbuild-kit chain), next-auth 5.0.0-beta.25 (email misdelivery, patched at beta.30), and postcss <8.5.10 (XSS via unescaped </style> in stringify output, pulled in via Next.js 16.2.10).
expectedBehavior: Dependencies with known high-severity advisories should be upgraded to patched versions promptly, especially the ORM used for every tenant-scoped query.
actualBehavior: drizzle-orm is pinned to ^0.38.0 (resolves to 0.38.4), below the 0.45.2 patched version.
reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm audit --audit-level=high  (also: pnpm audit, for the 4 additional moderate findings)
recommendation: Upgrade drizzle-orm to >=0.45.2 (check for breaking changes against drizzle-kit and schema code), upgrade next-auth to >=5.0.0-beta.30, and let drizzle-kit pull in a patched esbuild-kit/esbuild chain; postcss will resolve once Next.js patches its bundled dependency.
workaround: None; not yet exploited but exposure is real.

title: Form labels not programmatically associated with inputs (missing htmlFor/id) across core HR forms
role: HR Manager, Employee, Department Manager, Super Admin
description: Several of the app's oldest/core forms render plain `<label>` text with no `htmlFor`, paired with `<Input>`/`<select>` elements that have no `id`. This breaks the programmatic label-control relationship: clicking the label text does not focus the field, and screen readers announce the field with no accessible name (or fall back to the placeholder only). By contrast, newer forms (login, signup, recruitment/*, retention/*, offboarding/*, documents/upload) consistently pair `htmlFor` with matching `id`s, showing the pattern is known and used elsewhere in the codebase — it just wasn't applied to these forms.
expectedBehavior: Every `<label>` should carry `htmlFor` matching the paired input's `id` (or wrap the input), so assistive technology announces a clear field name and label-click focuses the control — as already done correctly in apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx.
actualBehavior: Labels and inputs are visually adjacent but have no id/htmlFor link in the forms listed in Evidence.
reproduction: 1. Open /employees/new, step to the form. 2. Inspect any field's label in devtools — no `for` attribute; input has no `id`. 3. With a screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver), tab into 'Full Name' — it is announced generically rather than as 'Full Name, edit text'.
recommendation: Add matching `id`/`htmlFor` pairs to all fields in the listed forms (or introduce a small `<FormField>` wrapper component in packages/ui that auto-generates and wires ids, then migrate these pages to it) to prevent regression.
workaround: Sighted mouse/touch users are unaffected; only assistive-technology users are impacted.

title: No absolute session lifetime and stale role/privileges in JWT (no revocation on role change or deactivation)
description: The JWT session uses a 30-minute rolling idle window (maxAge 30m, updateAge 5m) with NO absolute cap, so an active session can be extended indefinitely. The jwt callback only re-hydrates employeeId on later requests - it never re-reads the user's role or verifies the account still exists/is enabled. Consequently a role downgrade, a role removal, or an account deletion is not reflected until the idle window lapses (up to 30 min), and there is no server-side revocation because the strategy is stateless JWT. The users table has no 'disabled/status' column, so an operator cannot force-logout or lock out an active token at all.
expectedBehavior: Role/authorization changes and account deactivation should take effect on the next request (re-read role from DB in the jwt callback or check a token version / session revocation list), and sessions should have an absolute maximum lifetime.
actualBehavior: Role is copied only at first sign-in; later requests keep the old role. No absolute expiry, no revocation, no account-disabled concept.
reproduction: Sign in as a user, have an admin change that user's role to a lower one; the active session keeps admin capabilities until the 30-min idle window expires. Deleting the user does not invalidate the live token.
recommendation: Re-read role (and an is_active/token_version) from the DB in the jwt callback on each request, add an absolute session cap, and add an account-status column so operators can force revocation.
workaround: Reduce maxAge; instruct admins that role changes take effect after the idle window.

title: Account lockout silently disabled by schema drift - lockout columns absent from Drizzle schema
description: The durable account-lockout code reads/writes failed_login_attempts and locked_until via raw SQL wrapped in try/catch that swallows all errors and 'disables lockout until migrated' when the columns are missing. Those columns are defined ONLY in migration 0008; they are NOT present in the Drizzle schema (users.ts). Because CLAUDE.md documents `pnpm db:push` (schema-push) as a workflow, a push from the drifted schema can DROP failed_login_attempts/locked_until, silently turning brute-force lockout off with no error surfaced. Prior memory notes already flag attendance schema drift in this repo.
expectedBehavior: Lockout columns should be part of the source-of-truth Drizzle schema so db:push preserves them, and a missing-column condition should be logged loudly, not swallowed.
actualBehavior: Columns live only in a hand-written migration; the schema object omits them; failures are swallowed and lockout becomes a no-op.
reproduction: Run pnpm db:push from the current schema (which lacks the columns) against a DB that has them -> Drizzle proposes dropping failed_login_attempts/locked_until; after that, recordFailedAttempt/getLockState throw and are swallowed, so accounts never lock.
recommendation: Add failedLoginAttempts and lockedUntil to the Drizzle users schema so they are managed and preserved; replace the silent catch with a one-time warning log/metric.
workaround: Use db:migrate (not db:push) in all environments; verify columns exist post-deploy.

title: No audit trail on any expense mutation (create / approve / markPaid)
description: The audit helper writeAudit is used for payroll runs, reopens and settlements, but the expense router never calls it. Approvals and payouts of company money leave no who/when/old/new record, so the self-approval and over-broad-payout risks (BIZ-001/002) cannot be detected or investigated after the fact.
expectedBehavior: Every financial state change (approve, reject, pay) should write an audit_logs entry with actor, role, old/new status and amount, per the project's 'Audit log for all sensitive operations' rule.
actualBehavior: No writeAudit calls exist anywhere in expense.ts; the module is entirely un-audited.
reproduction: Approve and mark an expense paid, then inspect audit_logs: no rows are written for the action.
recommendation: Add writeAudit to approve, reject and markPaid (and ideally create/cancel), recording actor, role, amount and status transition.
workaround: None at the application layer; rely on DB-level change data capture if available.

title: All timestamps use `timestamp` (without time zone) — Riyadh (UTC+3) correctness risk for punch/approval/payroll times
description: The generated migration 0000 contains zero timestamptz columns; every createdAt/updatedAt and event timestamp (punch_in_at, punch_out_at, approved_at, paid_at, completed_at, sent_at, resolved_at, etc.) is `timestamp` without time zone. Serverless writers run in UTC while the business operates in Asia/Riyadh (UTC+3). Naive timestamps store whatever wall clock the writer used with no offset, so a punch recorded in UTC and displayed as Riyadh local (or vice versa) can be off by 3 hours — material for attendance late/early minutes, overtime, and payroll period boundaries. (Pure `date` columns for hire/termination/leave/period_month are acceptable.) Note inconsistency: hand-written migration 0002 uses timestamptz, so the codebase is mixed.
expectedBehavior: Instant-in-time columns should be timestamptz (stored as UTC, rendered in Asia/Riyadh), consistently across schema and migrations.
actualBehavior: timestamp without time zone everywhere in the Drizzle schema; timestamptz only in one ad-hoc migration.
reproduction: grep -c timestamptz packages/db/drizzle/0000_thin_freak.sql -> 0. Record a punch from a UTC serverless function, render in Riyadh -> 3h skew.
recommendation: Migrate instant columns to timestamptz and standardize on UTC storage + Asia/Riyadh presentation. If mass migration is too costly short-term, enforce a single writer timezone (UTC) app-wide and document it; long-term convert to timestamptz.
workaround: Ensure every writer sets time in UTC and every reader converts to Asia/Riyadh explicitly.

title: Field-encryption key falls back to a public hardcoded dev key outside NODE_ENV=production; no key rotation/versioning
description: deriveKeys() throws only when NODE_ENV === 'production'. In any other environment (preview, staging, CI, or a misconfigured prod where NODE_ENV isn't exactly 'production'), a missing FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY silently uses DEV_FALLBACK_KEY = 'hrms-dev-only-insecure-field-encryption-key', which is in source control. PII (iqama/passport/IBAN) written in such environments is effectively plaintext. Separately, the construction has no key-versioning/rotation: the marker is a static 'encv1:' and the key label is fixed 'v1'; rotating FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY changes every deterministic ciphertext and makes all previously stored values fail GCM auth on read (decryptField throws), with no dual-key/rewrap path.
expectedBehavior: Missing key should fail closed in every deployed environment (not just production). A key id should be embedded so keys can be rotated with a rewrap/dual-read window.
actualBehavior: Silent dev-key fallback for all non-production NODE_ENV; no rotation mechanism.
reproduction: Deploy a preview build with NODE_ENV!=='production' and no FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY; write an employee iqama; read the raw column -> ciphertext decryptable with the repo's DEV_FALLBACK_KEY. Rotate the key -> existing rows throw on read.
recommendation: Fail closed whenever the app is deployed (e.g. require the key unless an explicit local/test flag is set), never fall back to a committed key in any hosted environment. Add a key id to the marker (encv1:<keyid>:) and implement dual-read (old+new key) + background rewrap for rotation. Validate presence at startup via packages/config env.
workaround: Set FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY in every deployed environment including preview/staging.
estimatedEffort: M (fail-closed is S; rotation support is M)

title: Single flat Drizzle migration mixes public and all tenant tables into one unqualified schema; no per-tenant migration runner
description: drizzle.config.ts scans schema/**/*.ts (both public and tenant). The generated 0000_thin_freak.sql contains 78 CREATE TABLE statements — public tables (accounts, sessions, users, tenants, verification_tokens, invite_token_index) AND all tenant tables (employees, payslips, etc.) — with no schema qualifier and 106 ADD CONSTRAINT FKs, all in a single schema. drizzle-kit migrate/push targets the connection's default schema (public), so running it would create every tenant table in public. There is no per-tenant migration runner; ongoing schema evolution for tenants is done via ad-hoc DO-block or hardcoded migrations (DB-002), which is the root cause of the drift findings above.
expectedBehavior: A clear separation: public-schema migrations for shared tables, and a dedicated per-tenant migration/DDL pipeline replaying an authoritative bundle into each tenant_<hex> schema. drizzle-kit generate should not blend the two.
actualBehavior: One flat migration blends public + tenant tables; no mechanism keeps existing tenant schemas in sync with the Drizzle schema.
reproduction: grep 'CREATE TABLE' 0000_thin_freak.sql -> 78 tables incl. both public and tenant. Run drizzle-kit migrate against the dev DB -> tenant tables land in public.
recommendation: Split schema generation: keep drizzle migrations for public-schema tables only, and build a first-class per-tenant migration runner (ordered SQL bundle applied with search_path per tenant, tracked in a per-tenant migrations table). Regenerate provisioning DDL from the tenant schema. Remove tenant tables from the public-targeted migration path.
workaround: Never run db:migrate/db:push against the shared DB without a schema target; manage tenant DDL via scripts.

title: users.mfa_secret (TOTP seed) is stored plaintext, not wrapped in encryptedText
description: The users table stores mfaSecret as plain text. Unlike employee iqama/passport/IBAN, the MFA shared secret is not wrapped in the encryptedText codec, so the TOTP seed sits at rest in cleartext. Anyone able to read the row can reconstruct the user's one-time codes.
expectedBehavior: MFA/TOTP secrets should be encrypted at rest (or stored in a dedicated secret store), consistent with the SEC-008 encryption applied to other PII.
actualBehavior: mfa_secret is plaintext text.
reproduction: Read users.mfa_secret -> base32 TOTP seed in cleartext; feed to any TOTP generator to produce valid codes.
recommendation: Wrap mfa_secret with the encryptedText codec (or a KMS-backed secret store). Since MFA secrets need no equality/uniqueness, prefer a randomized-IV variant rather than the deterministic codec to avoid equality leakage. Backfill existing rows.
workaround: Restrict DB read access; rotate MFA enrollments if exposure suspected.

title: Audit log omits authentication, role-assignment, export, and PII-view events
description: writeAudit is invoked only for employee create/update/delete, payroll runs, settlement, and PDPL self-export. Security- and privacy-relevant events are NOT persisted to audit_logs: successful/failed logins and lockouts (only ephemeral console.warn), role assignment/changes via invites, MFA enable/disable, document/letter generation (PRIV-010), and PII/salary views. PDPL accountability and standard security monitoring expect durable auth and access-to-PII trails.
expectedBehavior: Login/logout, lockout, role changes, MFA changes, config changes, and PII/export access are written to a durable audit trail with actor/action/target/timestamp.
actualBehavior: Auth events are only console-logged; role/MFA/export/view events are not audited.
reproduction: Log in, assign a role via invite, generate a letter; query audit_logs — none of these appear.
recommendation: Emit audit_logs entries for auth (login/logout/lockout/MFA), role/permission changes, config changes, and PII exports/views. Keep the existing tenant-scoped, read-only audit read surface.
workaround: Ship auth events to a durable log store as an interim measure.

title: employee.getById / employee.list expose salary + payslips to roles lacking payroll:view_company; getById is not department-scoped
description: employee.list and employee.getById are companyProcedure (any staff role). The employees row carries plaintext salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport columns and no column projection is applied, so recruiter and department_manager — who hold people:view_company but NOT payroll:view_company — read every employee's base salary. Worse, employee.getById additionally loads the payslips relation (full pay history) and, unlike employee.list, applies NO department scoping. A department_manager can therefore pass any employeeId to getById and read that employee's complete record plus payslip financials, defeating the SEC-013 department-scope intent for reads. The prior audit's permission matrix marked employee.list/getById as 'Secure', missing this.
expectedBehavior: Salary columns and the payslips relation should be gated to PAYROLL_VIEW_ROLES / payroll:view_company (projected out for other roles), and employee.getById should apply the same getManagedDepartmentIds scoping as employee.list for department_manager.
actualBehavior: recruiter and department_manager receive salaryBasic/Housing/Transport for all employees via list; department_manager receives any employee's full record + payslips via getById with no scope check.
reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call employee.list -> response rows include salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport. Login as department_manager -> call employee.getById with an employeeId outside your department -> returns that employee's full record + payslips.
recommendation: Add a column projection to employee.list/getById that omits salary fields for roles without payroll:view_company, gate the payslips relation on PAYROLL_VIEW_ROLES, and apply getManagedDepartmentIds scoping inside getById for department_manager.
workaround: None client-side; server change required.

title: Required-field markers are visual-only and validation errors are not announced to assistive technology
description: Required fields are marked with a plain "*" in the label text, but the underlying ``/`
expectedBehavior: Required inputs should carry `required`/`aria-required="true"`; the error summary should be in a `role="alert"` (or `aria-live="assertive"`) container so screen-reader users are told immediately when submission fails, matching apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx:78-85.
actualBehavior: Submitting either form with missing/invalid data updates a plain div with the error text; a screen-reader user who has already moved focus away from the form is not notified anything happened.
reproduction: 1. Open /leave/new, submit with no dates selected. 2. Error text appears visually but a screen reader user focused elsewhere gets no announcement.
recommendation: Add `role="alert"` to error containers and `required`/`aria-required` to fields already validated as mandatory in handleSubmit.
workaround: Sighted users see the red banner directly below the button they just clicked.

title: pnpm lint fails with 372 errors (almost entirely no-explicit-any / no-non-null-assertion) confined to the web app
description: `pnpm lint` fails (turbo reports @hrms-app/web#lint exited 1). All 16 other packages (auth, config, date, db, demo, documents, email, leave, llm, payroll, qiwa, queue, ui, validators, etc.) lint clean. Only apps/web fails, with 387 problems (372 errors, 15 warnings) across ~84 files. The dominant rule is @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any (the large majority of the 372 errors — 295 raw `: any`/`as any` occurrences were found across apps/web by static grep), followed by @typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion (~30+ occurrences, e.g. attendance/me/page.tsx, payroll pages, leave.ts, invite.ts) and a smaller number of no-unused-vars.
expectedBehavior: `pnpm lint` should exit 0 across all packages, per the project's own ESLint config which forbids `any` and non-null assertions.
actualBehavior: @hrms-app/web#lint exits with code 1; 372 errors reported.
reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm lint
recommendation: Triage by rule: replace `any` in router callback signatures (e.g. attendance.ts:60-61, department.ts:11, leave.ts:82) with the inferred Drizzle relation types; replace non-null assertions with explicit null checks or narrow the type upstream. Given the volume, tackle incrementally per-router rather than one large PR.
workaround: None; lint is currently red.

title: File upload storage path traversal via unsanitized category field
description: In apps/web/app/api/upload/route.ts the object-storage key is built as `${tenantId}/${category}/${timestamp}_${safeFileName}`. The file name is sanitized (safeFileName), but the client-supplied `category` string is used raw. A value such as `../<otherTenantId>` would resolve the stored object outside the caller's tenant prefix within the Supabase bucket, breaking tenant isolation of stored files.
expectedBehavior: The storage path segment derived from category must be validated against an allowlist (or sanitized the same way the filename is) so it cannot contain path separators or traversal sequences.
actualBehavior: category is concatenated into the storage path without sanitization; `../` or slash characters allow escaping the tenant folder.
reproduction: POST multipart to /api/upload with field category set to `../<victimTenantId>/docs` and observe the object written under the victim tenant's prefix.
recommendation: Validate category against a fixed enum (e.g. policies, contracts, ids) or apply the same [^a-zA-Z0-9._-] sanitization used for the filename; reject null/empty category.
workaround: Enforce an allowlist of category values server-side.

title: MFA is opt-in only and not enforced by any policy (privileged roles can operate without MFA)
description: TOTP MFA is only enforced when the individual user has already enrolled a secret (if (user.mfaSecret)). There is no organization policy requiring MFA for high-privilege roles (super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin), and nothing in signup/onboarding drives enrollment. A compromised password alone is sufficient for full admin access on any account that has not voluntarily enrolled.
expectedBehavior: Privileged roles should be required to enrol MFA (block or step-up until enrolled), configurable per tenant.
actualBehavior: MFA gate is skipped entirely for any account without mfaSecret; no role-based enforcement exists.
reproduction: Create/sign in as an hr_manager without enrolling MFA; login succeeds with password only.
recommendation: Add a tenant policy that requires MFA for privileged roles; block access (or force enrollment) until a secret is set.
workaround: Operationally require admins to enrol MFA.

title: In-memory middleware rate limiting is per-instance and ineffective on serverless/multi-instance
description: Rate limiting is backed by a module-level in-memory Map. On Vercel/serverless (and any horizontally-scaled deploy) each instance has its own map and the map resets on cold start, so the 5/min login limit and other limits are enforced only per-instance - an attacker rotating across instances or after cold starts largely evades them. The durable per-account lockout mitigates this for the NextAuth path, but the standalone /api/auth/login route (AUTH-002) has neither. Redis (Upstash) is already configured in env but not used for rate limiting.
expectedBehavior: Rate limiting for auth endpoints should use a shared store (Redis/Upstash) so limits are global across instances.
actualBehavior: Limits use a per-process Map that resets on cold start.
reproduction: Send login attempts spread across cold starts / instances; aggregate throughput exceeds the intended 5/min.
recommendation: Move auth rate limiting to Upstash Redis (already in env) keyed by IP and by account; keep the durable DB lockout as defense-in-depth.
workaround: Add a WAF/edge rate limit in front of /api/auth/*.

title: Leave balance can be over-drawn: balance is checked at submission but not re-validated at approval
description: The available-balance check runs only in create, against the balance at submission time. Two (or more) non-overlapping requests can each pass because the balance is not decremented until approval. At approval, adjustLeaveBalance decrements with Math.max(0, ...), which silently floors a negative balance to 0 instead of rejecting, so the employee is granted more paid leave than their entitlement.
expectedBehavior: Approval should re-validate the current balance and refuse (or partially grant) when the request exceeds the remaining balance; balance must never be allowed to go negative silently.
actualBehavior: No balance re-check at approval; decrement clamps to 0, hiding the over-draw.
reproduction: With balance = 10 days, create request A (7 days) and request B (7 days, non-overlapping) — both pass the create check. Approve both. Final balance shows 0 instead of -4; employee took 14 of 10 days.
recommendation: Move/duplicate the balance check into updateStatus at the approval boundary (reject if days > current balance) and remove the silent Math.max floor (or log when a floor would occur).
workaround: HR manually reconciles balances before approving stacked requests.

title: Leave approvals have no segregation of duties: an approver can approve their own leave
description: updateStatus authorises super_admin, hr_manager and department_manager to approve leave. There is no check that the approver is not the leave's own employee. A department_manager who heads their own department (dept scope check passes because their own department is in the managed set) can approve their own leave request; hr_manager/super_admin can approve their own outright.
expectedBehavior: The approver of a leave request must not be the requesting employee; self-approval should be blocked and routed to a higher authority.
actualBehavior: Only role and (for department_manager) department scope are checked; actor != subject is not.
reproduction: As a department_manager whose own employee record sits in a department they head, create a leave request (bound to self) then call updateStatus { id, data:{ status:'approved' } }. It succeeds.
recommendation: In updateStatus, load the request's employeeId and reject when it equals the caller's employeeId (for the approval transition); require escalation to a higher approver.
workaround: Policy control + monitor approvedByUserId vs employee.

title: No data-subject rectification / erasure / consent-withdrawal endpoints (PDPL rights incomplete)
description: A PDPL data-subject access (export) right is implemented (employee.exportMyData, audited). However there is no self-service or documented workflow for the right to correction, the right to destruction/erasure, or withdrawal of consent. Employee deletion is soft-delete only (status=terminated) which by design retains all PII indefinitely, and no anonymisation/retention-expiry job exists. Saudi PDPL grants data subjects rights to access, correction, and destruction; only access is covered in code.
expectedBehavior: Provide (or document a controlled process for) correction, erasure/anonymisation, consent withdrawal, and a defined retention/disposal schedule.
actualBehavior: Only exportMyData exists; soft-delete retains PII with no erasure/anonymisation path.
reproduction: Search routers for erasure/rectification/consent-withdrawal procedures; none exist.
recommendation: Add correction and erasure/anonymisation workflows (audited), a consent-withdrawal path, and a documented retention & disposal policy. Legal review required to define lawful retention periods (payroll/tax records vs erasable PII).
workaround: Document a manual, audited DSR handling process in the interim.

title: Salary certificate / experience letter generation exposes decrypted iqama + full salary and is not audited
description: document.generateLetter builds a salary certificate / experience letter containing the employee's decrypted national ID (iqama) and full basic/housing/transport salary. This is a sensitive PII/financial export, but no writeAudit call is made, so there is no record of who generated a document containing another person's iqama and salary or when. writeAudit is used for employee/payroll/settlement mutations but not for this read/export.
expectedBehavior: Every generation/export of a document containing another person's PII/salary is audited (actor, subject, type, timestamp).
actualBehavior: generateLetter returns HTML with iqama + salary and writes no audit entry.
reproduction: Call document.generateLetter; check audit_logs — no entry recorded for the export.
recommendation: Add writeAudit('document.generate', entityType 'employee', subject id, {type}) to generateLetter; consider the same for any PII-bearing read/export.
workaround: N/A

title: leave.runAccrual issues one sequential DB write per employee×leaveType combination instead of a bulk operation
description: The `runAccrual` mutation computes accrual results for every active employee × leave type in memory, then loops over the results issuing one `await ctx.db.insert(...)` or `await ctx.db.update(...)` per row sequentially inside a for-loop, rather than batching the writes.
expectedBehavior: Bulk insert/update (e.g. a single multi-row insert with onConflict upsert, or Promise.all with a bounded concurrency) so the accrual run scales sub-linearly with employee count.
actualBehavior: for (const result of results) { await ctx.db.insert(...) } / await ctx.db.update(...) — strictly sequential, one round trip per employee per leave type.
reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/leave.ts lines 319-386 (runAccrual mutation).
recommendation: Batch inserts with a single multi-row INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, or chunk the loop with Promise.all in small batches (e.g. 20 at a time) to reduce round trips while bounding connection usage.
workaround: Fine at current demo/pilot scale.

title: No timeout or retry on any external HTTP call (Qiwa gov API, Gemini, Claude)
page: AI assistant chat, Qiwa sync features
description: Every external fetch() call in the Qiwa client (packages/qiwa/src/index.ts, 8 call sites: auth token, employees, contracts, contract status, health check) and both LLM providers (packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts:65, packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts:56) is a bare `await fetch(...)` with no AbortController/signal timeout and no retry/backoff logic.
expectedBehavior: External calls to third-party/government APIs and LLM providers should have a bounded timeout (e.g. 10-30s via AbortController) and at least one retry for transient network errors, so a slow upstream cannot hang a user-facing request indefinitely.
actualBehavior: fetch() calls have no `signal`/timeout option anywhere in the qiwa or llm packages; a hung upstream connection will hang the calling tRPC procedure until the platform's own request timeout (if any) kicks in.
reproduction: grep -n "fetch(\|timeout\|AbortController\|retry" packages/qiwa/src/index.ts packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts — no timeout/retry keyword matches at any call site.
recommendation: Add an AbortController-based timeout (e.g. 15s for LLM, 20s for Qiwa) to every external fetch, and a single retry with backoff for network-level failures (not for 4xx application errors).
workaround: The AI chat path already has a try/catch fallback to a stub reply for context-fetching, which limits blast radius for that one call; the main completion call itself has no fallback.

title: Several tRPC routers with real business logic have no dedicated unit/integration tests (attendance, expense, compliance, department, settlement, notification)
module: Attendance / Expense / Compliance / Department / Settlement / Notification
page: Attendance, Expenses, Compliance, Departments, Settlements, Notifications
description: apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts (788 lines: shifts, shift assignments, punches, exceptions, monthly reports, GPS subtree) has no matching unit/integration test file; it is only touched indirectly by two Playwright UI specs (attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts), which test rendering, not the underlying query/business logic (overtime calc, late/absence classification, subtree building). expense.ts, compliance.ts, department.ts, settlement.ts, and notification.ts routers similarly have no dedicated test file.
expectedBehavior: Routers implementing calculations (overtime, late minutes) or RBAC-scoped visibility rules (expense.ts's SEC-004 remediation for pending-approval visibility) should have logic-level tests independent of the UI.
actualBehavior: Only e2e/UI-level coverage exists for attendance; no coverage at all for expense/compliance/department/settlement/notification routers.
reproduction: find . -name '*.test.ts' | grep -iE 'attendance|expense|compliance|department|settlement|notification' — no matches beyond leave/payroll's own attendance-adjacent overtime tests in packages/payroll.
recommendation: Prioritize a test for expense.ts's role-scoped visibility (given it references a prior IDOR fix) and attendance.ts's overtime/late calculation before adding new features to these routers.
workaround: None.

title: Incomplete SEC-006 fix: attendance company reads and leave company reads remain companyProcedure (recruiter over-exposure)
url: attendance.list, attendance.monthlyReport, attendance.exceptions, attendance.assignment.list, leave.request.list, leave.balance.list
description: The Phase 2 SEC-006 remediation applied requireCapability to recruitment, retention, settlement, document and ai reads, but did NOT touch the attendance and leave company-wide reads. attendance.list/monthlyReport/exceptions/assignment.list and leave.request.list/balance.list are still companyProcedure, which authorizes any non-employee/candidate staff role. The capability model grants attendance:view_company and leave:view_company to super_admin/hr_manager/hr_specialist/payroll_admin/department_manager but NOT recruiter. A recruiter therefore reads all-employee attendance (attendance.list embeds each employee row and last workLocation) and all-employee leave, outside their capability set. The 03-permission-matrix.md itself flagged these as 'Over-permissive (recruiter)' — they were never closed.
expectedBehavior: attendance.list/monthlyReport/exceptions/assignment.list should be requireCapability('attendance:view_company') and leave.request.list/balance.list requireCapability('leave:view_company'), consistent with the rest of Phase 2.
actualBehavior: recruiter (no attendance/leave company capability) can read every employee's attendance and leave records.
reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call attendance.list or leave.request.list -> returns company-wide records despite recruiter lacking attendance:view_company / leave:view_company.
recommendation: Swap companyProcedure -> requireCapability('attendance:view_company') on the four attendance reads and requireCapability('leave:view_company') on leave.request.list/balance.list (mirror the getSubtree fix already applied).
workaround: None client-side.

title: expense.markPaid callable by any staff role (finance action on companyProcedure)
description: expense.markPaid transitions an approved expense to 'paid' (a finance close-out) but is guarded only by companyProcedure, so every non-employee/candidate staff role — including recruiter and department_manager, who hold no finance/payroll capability — can mark expenses paid. The in-code comment claims 'Only HR / super_admin can do this', which the guard does not enforce. SEC-004 fixed the read path but left this write over-permissive; the 03-permission-matrix.md flagged it as 'Over-permissive (any staff role)'.
expectedBehavior: markPaid should be requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) / requireCapability('payroll:view_company') to match the finance-close intent.
actualBehavior: recruiter and department_manager can move expenses to paid state.
reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call expense.markPaid on an approved expense id -> status becomes 'paid'.
recommendation: Change expense.markPaid to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) (the EXPENSE_COMPANY_ROLES already defined in this file) or requireCapability.
workaround: None client-side.

title: Alternate login route /api/auth/login bypasses account lockout and rate limiting (brute-force oracle)
description: POST /api/auth/login performs a bcrypt password comparison against users.passwordHash and returns ok+user on success, but it bypasses every protection the NextAuth authorize() callback provides: the durable per-account lockout, the MFA gate, and the login rate-limit. The middleware only rate-limits /api/auth/callback/credentials (5/min) and general /api/auth (loose), and /api/auth/login is NOT in the middleware matcher at all, so no edge throttle applies. The route does not set a session cookie, so it is a password-verification oracle rather than a session bypass — but it enables unthrottled offline-style online password guessing that the account-lockout control (praised in the prior audit) was meant to stop.
expectedBehavior: There should be a single authentication path (NextAuth credentials) that enforces lockout, MFA and rate-limiting; ad-hoc credential-check routes should not exist, or must apply the same lockout/rate-limit logic.
actualBehavior: Unlimited unauthenticated password guesses against any account with no lockout, MFA or throttle.
reproduction: Script POST /api/auth/login with a fixed email and many passwords -> no lockout or 429 is ever returned; failed_login_attempts/locked_until are never consulted.
recommendation: Remove /api/auth/login if unused; otherwise route it through the same authorize() logic (lockout + MFA + rate-limit) and add it to the middleware matcher's login rate-limit branch.
workaround: Add /api/auth/login to the login rate-limit matcher and block at edge.

title: Focus outline removed with no replacement on Super Admin tenant filter input
description: Across the codebase, every other instance of `focus:outline-none` is paired with a replacement indicator (`focus:ring-2`, `focus:border-*`, etc. — 28 of 29 occurrences checked). The tenant filter input on the Super Admin page is the sole exception: it removes the native outline and supplies no visible focus state at all.
expectedBehavior: Keyboard users tabbing to the filter input should see a visible focus indicator (ring/border/box-shadow), consistent with every other input in the app.
actualBehavior: Tabbing into the field shows no visible change; the input is indistinguishable from its unfocused state.
reproduction: 1. Open /super-admin as a super_admin user. 2. Tab through the page until focus reaches the 'Filter by name, CR, schema' input. 3. No focus ring or border change is visible.
recommendation: Add `focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-600` (or equivalent) matching the sibling filter/search inputs elsewhere in the app.
workaround: Mouse users unaffected.

title: Hand-rolled "Save Map View" modal lacks dialog semantics, focus trap and Escape-to-close
description: The app already ships an accessible modal primitive (packages/ui/src/ui/dialog.tsx, built on @radix-ui/react-dialog, with built-in focus trap, Escape handling and aria-modal) and uses it correctly elsewhere (apps/web/app/(dashboard)/attendance/portal/page.tsx:249-328). The Guide Map page's "Save This View" flow instead renders a plain `fixed inset-0` overlay `<div>` with no `role="dialog"`, no `aria-modal="true"`, no focus trap, and no keydown handler for Escape.
expectedBehavior: Opening 'Save This View' should trap focus inside the dialog, be announced as a dialog to screen readers, and close on Escape — matching the Punch Dialog pattern on /attendance/portal.
actualBehavior: Tab can move focus out of the overlay into the page behind it; Escape does nothing; no dialog role is exposed.
reproduction: 1. Open /attendance/guide-map. 2. Click 'Save This View'. 3. Press Tab repeatedly — focus escapes into the sidebar/header behind the overlay. 4. Press Escape — nothing happens.
recommendation: Replace the plain overlay with `Dialog`/`DialogContent` from @hrms-app/ui, consistent with attendance/portal/page.tsx.
workaround: Mouse/touch users can click Cancel or click outside conceptually (though click-outside isn't wired either, since it's not a real dialog).

title: In-app help chat has no aria-live region for new messages
description: The floating Taazur Help chat appends new assistant/user messages to a scrolling `<div>` with no `aria-live` attribute. A screen-reader user who submits a question and then reviews other parts of the page will not be notified when the AI's reply arrives.
